cultivar.origin_location: Brownwood, TX (cross); adapted for northern pecan belt
cultivar.description: Northern-adapted USDA pecan with outstanding scab resistance and consistent production. Cross of Major x Shoshoni, widely regarded as the best all-around cultivar for zones 6-8.
cultivar.notes: Protogynous (Type II). 74 nuts/lb. One of the most recommended cultivars for permaculture plantings in the transition zone between northern and southern pecan regions. Female parent of several newer USDA releases.

traits.flavor_profile: Rich, buttery flavor with golden-colored kernels. Wide dorsal grooves.
traits.vigor: Moderate. Easier to manage than many northern selections.

cultivar.description: Very early ripening pecan with large nuts and high kernel percentage. Widely available and popular for home orchards in zones 6-9.
cultivar.notes: Protandrous (Type I). 44 nuts/lb. Susceptible to scab — best in drier climates or where fungicide is applied. Very early maturity (mid-September) makes it viable further north than its zone rating suggests.

traits.flavor_profile: Light-colored kernel with excellent eating quality. Mild, sweet flavor.

cultivar.description: Promising northern pecan with excellent scab resistance and good nut quality. Named and released from a seedling tree in western Illinois.
cultivar.notes: Protandrous (Type I). ~62 nuts/lb. Easier to train than Kanza as a young tree with more naturally spreading growth habit. Possible Major parentage. Good partner for Kanza (Type II).

traits.flavor_profile: Light-colored, well-filled kernels with sweet, rich flavor.
traits.vigor: Moderate to vigorous. Naturally spreading growth habit.

cultivar.breeder: Native selection by Mrs. L.B. Major
cultivar.origin_location: Henderson County, KY (Green River / Ohio River confluence)
cultivar.description: Historic northern pecan standard. Native selection from the northernmost pecan range with outstanding cold hardiness and reliable production. Female parent of Kanza and Osage.
cultivar.notes: Protandrous (Type I). 78 nuts/lb. Small nut but dependable cropper. One of the oldest named northern pecan cultivars. Foundational variety for USDA breeding program.

traits.flavor_profile: Cream to golden kernels with broad, shallow dorsal grooves. Good flavor.
traits.vigor: Moderate. Slow to come into bearing.
traits.tree_form: Large, spreading crown typical of native pecan

cultivar.description: Northern pecan with good nut size for the region and reliable production. Open-pollinated seedling selected at the Kansas Pecan Experiment Field.
cultivar.notes: Protogynous (Type II). ~70 nuts/lb. Oval elliptic nuts with ridges on shell. Golden kernels. Good pollinizer for Type I cultivars like Major and Hark.

traits.flavor_profile: Golden-colored kernels with wide dorsal grooves. Pleasant, sweet flavor.

cultivar.breeder: Selection from wild stand (Ed Yates planting)
cultivar.description: Hardy northern pecan from Indiana with distinctive flat nut shape and thick shucks. Reliable producer in zones 5-8 with good cold tolerance.
cultivar.notes: Protogynous (Type II). ~68 nuts/lb. Strongly flattened nut shape with prominent wing ridges on sutures. Dark colored shells. Most likely an open-pollinated seedling.

traits.flavor_profile: Well-filled kernels. Sweet, slightly richer flavor than typical southern pecans.

cultivar.description: Consistent-yielding northern pecan from Missouri with good scab resistance. Named for Jerrell Shepherd who purchased the original farm in 1978.
cultivar.notes: Protandrous (Type I). 65 nuts/lb. Found on the Vernon Munson farm along the Grand River floodplain. Scions grafted at Shepherd Farms near Clifton Hills, MO. Good complement to Kanza and Posey (Type II).

traits.flavor_profile: Well-filled kernels with good cracking quality and pleasant flavor.

cultivar.breeder: A.S. Colby / J.C. McDaniel, University of Illinois
cultivar.description: Classic far-northern pecan and widely used seedstock for the northern pecan industry. Native seedling selection with proven cold hardiness to zone 5.
cultivar.notes: Protogynous (Type II). ~60 nuts/lb. Medium-large oval nut. Grafted form is thin-shelled and heavy-producing. Often used as seedling rootstock. 160 days to ripen.

traits.flavor_profile: Golden kernels, sweet and meaty. Medium-size oblong shape.

cultivar.description: Ultra-northern pecan from Iowa adapted to the shortest growing seasons. Found along the Mississippi River on Jim Mullahy's farm near Burlington.
cultivar.notes: Protogynous (Type II). ~79 nuts/lb. Small nut but extremely cold-hardy. One of the most northern-adapted named pecan cultivars. Good pollinizer for Hark and Shepherd.

traits.flavor_profile: Small but well-filled kernels with sweet flavor typical of northern pecans.

cultivar.description: Northern pecan selected for earliness of ripening, hardiness, productivity, and nut cracking quality. From a native grove in Missouri.
cultivar.notes: Suited for zones 5b-8. Selected for overall performance in short-season northern environments. Available from several specialty nut tree nurseries.

traits.flavor_profile: Good cracking quality with sweet, well-filled kernels.

cultivar.description: Northern pecan recommended for short-season areas in zones 5-8. Known for early ripening and reliable kernel filling even in cooler summers.
cultivar.notes: One of the earliest-ripening northern pecans. Available from Grimo Nut Nursery and other specialty sources.

traits.flavor_profile: Well-filled kernels with mild, sweet pecan flavor.

cultivar.description: One of the most popular named shagbark hickory selections. Medium-sized nut with good cracking quality from Ohio.
cultivar.notes: Late ripening; nuts often drop in the husk requiring hand-husking. Alternate bearing with good crops 2 out of 3 years. Among the most widely grafted shagbark cultivars.

traits.flavor_profile: Rich, sweet hickory flavor. Classic shagbark taste prized for baking and eating fresh.
traits.vigor: Vigorous. Slow to establish but long-lived.
traits.tree_form: Large upright tree with characteristic shaggy bark

cultivar.description: One of the finest shagbark hickory selections. Exceptionally large nut approaching shellbark size, with thin shell that cracks into whole halves. Weevil-resistant and very productive.
cultivar.notes: Often cracks out in halves and large pieces. Weevil-resistant. Excellent flavor. Available from Grimo Nut Nursery, Rock Bridge Trees, and other specialty sources. Good pollinizer for other shagbarks.

traits.flavor_profile: Excellent rich hickory flavor. Thin shell cracks easily into halves.

cultivar.description: Excellent cracking shagbark hickory selected by the Pennsylvania Nut Growers Association. Winner of cracking contests with well-filled kernels.
cultivar.notes: Among the most popular current shagbark cultivars alongside Yoder and Wilcox. Nuts crack out well. Available from specialty nut nurseries.

traits.flavor_profile: Rich hickory flavor. Well-filled kernels that crack cleanly.

cultivar.description: Popular shagbark hickory selection known for reliable production and good nut quality. One of the three most widely propagated shagbark cultivars.
cultivar.notes: Among the most popular current shagbark cultivars alongside Yoder and Porter. Available from Grimo Nut Nursery and other specialty sources.

traits.flavor_profile: Classic shagbark hickory flavor. Sweet, rich, and distinctive.

cultivar.description: Historic shellbark hickory selection discovered by Fayette Etter along a creek in Pennsylvania. Named for the Keystone State. Original tree lost to flooding, preserved through grafting.
cultivar.notes: Fayette Etter rescued scion wood before the original tree was destroyed in a flood. Very large, sweet nuts. One of the most prized shellbark selections. Limited nursery availability.

traits.flavor_profile: Very large, sweet shellbark nut. Rich flavor superior to most hickories.
traits.vigor: Moderate to vigorous. Slow to establish.
traits.tree_form: Large upright tree, less shaggy bark than C. ovata

cultivar.description: Highly productive shellbark hickory with lateral bearing habit and high weevil resistance. Very large nut that is well-filled and cracks fairly easily.
cultivar.notes: Lateral bearing habit makes it one of the most productive shellbark selections. Mid to late October ripening. Discovered by Fayette Etter in Franklin County, PA.

traits.flavor_profile: Large, well-filled nut with sweet shellbark flavor. Cracks fairly easily.

cultivar.description: Precocious hican with very large nuts resembling jumbo shellbark in size but with elongated pecan-like shape. More productive than most shellbark hickories and reportedly self-fertile.
cultivar.notes: Self-fertile — unusual among hicans, most of which need cross-pollination. Nuts crack easier than hickory but not as easily as pecan. Available from Stark Bros and Nolin River Nut Tree Nursery. Size similar to jumbo shellbark with definite pecan-like ridging.

traits.flavor_profile: Intermediate between pecan and hickory. Rich, complex flavor with pecan sweetness and hickory depth.
traits.vigor: Vigorous. Faster growth than pure hickory.
traits.tree_form: Large, upright tree with pecan-like form

cultivar.description: Hardy, prolific hican (pecan x shagbark hickory) with good cracking quality. One of the best hican selections for northern food forests.
cultivar.notes: Pecan x shagbark hickory cross. Among the best hicans alongside Dooley. Hardy, prolific, and cracks well. Available from Rock Bridge Trees, Nolin River, and other specialty nurseries.

traits.flavor_profile: Pecan-like sweetness with hickory richness. Good cracking quality.
traits.vigor: Vigorous. Grows faster than pure hickory parents.
traits.tree_form: Large spreading tree, intermediate between pecan and hickory form

cultivar.description: Productive hican with qualities similar to Burton — hardy, prolific, and good cracking. Pecan parentage from Oklahoma combined with hickory hardiness.
cultivar.notes: Often listed alongside Burton as one of the two best hicans. Sometimes crossed with Burton to produce Dooley-Burton hybrid seedlings. Available from specialty nut tree nurseries.

traits.flavor_profile: Pecan-like flavor with hickory undertones. Well-filled kernels.

cultivar.description: Bitternut hickory x pecan hybrid ('bitcan') with extra cold hardiness and early ripening. Combines pecan tree form and flavor with bitternut's extreme northern range.
cultivar.notes: Bitternut x pecan parentage gives this cultivar hardiness beyond typical pecan or hican range. Discovered by A.N. Abbott in Morrison, Illinois. The bitternut parent (C. cordiformis) is the most cold-hardy Carya species.

traits.flavor_profile: Pecan-like flavor, milder than shagbark hicans. Some bitternut influence may be detectable.

cultivar.description: Canadian shagbark hickory selection from Grimo Nut Nursery. Seedling of Neilson with similar large nut size and good cracking quality. Proven in cold Ontario winters.
cultivar.notes: Seedling of Neilson selection. Large nut for a shagbark with good cracking quality. Available from Grimo Nut Nursery. Excellent choice for Canadian and northern US food forests.

traits.flavor_profile: Sweet, rich hickory flavor. Large kernel for a shagbark.

cultivar.description: Ultra-northern pecan adapted to zone 6 and farther north. Wild stand selection with proven cold hardiness for short-season permaculture plantings.
cultivar.notes: One of the hardiest northern pecan selections alongside Mullahy and Martzahn. Small nut typical of ultra-northern pecans. Available from specialty northern nut tree nurseries.

traits.flavor_profile: Small but sweet, well-filled kernels. Typical northern pecan quality.
